verses.commentary: You, therefore, have no excuse. Paul turns from Gentile sin (chapter 1) to Jewish sin. The one who judges others condemns himself because he does the same things. Hypocrisy is judging others for sins you commit yourself. Self-righteousness blinds us to our own guilt.
God's judgment is based on truth, not appearances or self-perception. God sees reality. Human judgment is often partial and hypocritical; God's judgment is thorough and just.

Do you think you will escape God's judgment? False security comes from judging others while committing the same sins. Comparing ourselves to others rather than to God's standard breeds delusion. No one escapes God's true evaluation.

Showing contempt for God's kindness, forbearance, and patience misunderstands their purpose. God's kindness is intended to lead to repentance, not presumption. Delaying judgment is mercy, not indifference. Misinterpreting patience as approval hardens the heart.

Stubbornness and an unrepentant heart store up wrath for the day of God's wrath. Present impenitence accumulates future judgment. The day when God's righteous judgment will be revealed is certain. Refusing to repent compounds guilt.

God will repay each person according to what they have done (Psalm 62:12, Proverbs 24:12). This is the principle of divine justice. Final judgment is based on works, which reveal the reality of faith. Works are evidence, not cause, of salvation.

To those who by persistence in doing good seek glory, honor, and immortality, He will give eternal life. Perseverance in good works demonstrates genuine faith. Seeking God's glory, not self-glory, marks true believers. Eternal life is the reward for those whose lives prove their faith.

For those who are self-seeking, reject the truth, and follow evil, there will be wrath and anger. Self-seeking (eritheia, ἐριθεία) implies selfish ambition. Rejecting truth and following evil reveal an unregenerate heart. Wrath is the certain outcome for the impenitent.

Trouble and distress for every human being who does evil: first for the Jew, then for the Gentile. Privilege increases responsibility. Jews had greater revelation, so greater accountability. Universal judgment applies to all, but order reflects covenantal history.

Glory, honor, and peace for everyone who does good: first for the Jew, then for the Gentile. Blessing follows righteousness. The order (Jew first, then Gentile) applies to both judgment and salvation, reflecting God's historical dealings.

God does not show favoritism (prosōpolēmpsia, προσωποληψία, partiality). Ethnic identity, religious heritage, and social status do not sway God's judgment. He judges with perfect impartiality based on truth.

All who sin apart from the law will perish apart from the law; all who sin under the law will be judged by the law. Gentiles without the Mosaic law are still accountable. Jews with the law are judged by it. Possession of the law does not excuse disobedience; it increases accountability.

Not those who hear the law are righteous in God's sight, but those who obey the law will be declared righteous. Hearing without doing is worthless. James 1:22-25 echoes this principle. Paul is not teaching salvation by works but exposing the failure of those who have the law but do not keep it.

Gentiles who do not have the law yet do by nature things required by the law are a law for themselves. Conscience and natural moral awareness reflect God's law written on the heart. Gentiles are not without moral knowledge; they have internal moral awareness.

The requirements of the law are written on their hearts; their consciences bear witness. Conscience either accuses or defends. Internal moral awareness testifies to God's law. This is not sufficient for salvation but demonstrates universal moral accountability.

Judgment will take place on the day when God judges people's secrets through Jesus Christ. Nothing is hidden from God. Jesus Christ is the agent of judgment (John 5:22, Acts 17:31). Paul's gospel includes the reality of coming judgment.

If you call yourself a Jew, rely on the law, and boast in God. Paul now addresses Jewish presumption directly. The privileges of being a Jew are real, but they do not guarantee salvation. Boasting in God while disobeying Him is hypocrisy.

Knowing God's will and approving what is superior because instructed by the law. Knowledge of God's will is a privilege. Moral clarity comes from Scripture. But knowledge without obedience is judgment, not salvation.

Convinced that you are a guide for the blind, a light for those in the dark. Jewish self-perception was as spiritual guides to Gentiles. This role was legitimate (Isaiah 42:6-7), but only if lived out faithfully. Claiming to guide others while being blind yourself is tragic irony.

An instructor of the foolish, a teacher of little children, because you have in the law the embodiment of knowledge and truth. The law contains true knowledge. Teachers must practice what they teach. Having truth is not the same as living truth.

You who teach others, do you not teach yourself? The question exposes hypocrisy. Instructing others while disobeying is self-condemnation. You who preach against stealing, do you steal? The inconsistency between profession and practice is damning.

You who say people should not commit adultery, do you commit adultery? You who abhor idols, do you rob temples? Even those who condemn idolatry can be guilty of sacrilege. Robbing temples may refer to taking what is consecrated to God or profiting from pagan temples.

You who boast in the law, do you dishonor God by breaking the law? Boasting in the law while disobeying it dishonors God. Pride in religious identity without obedience is empty. The law magnifies sin rather than covering it.

God's name is blasphemed among the Gentiles because of you (Isaiah 52:5, Ezekiel 36:20-23). Jewish disobedience caused Gentiles to mock God. When God's people act like the world, they discredit God's name. Hypocrisy is a stumbling block to unbelievers.

Circumcision has value if you observe the law, but if you break the law, you have become as though you had not been circumcised. The sign is meaningless without the reality it represents. External religious ritual without internal obedience is worthless. Disobedience nullifies the covenant sign.

If those who are not circumcised keep the law's requirements, will they not be regarded as though they were circumcised? Obedience matters more than ritual. A Gentile who obeys is more acceptable than a Jew who disobeys. God values heart reality over external markers.

The one who is not circumcised physically yet obeys the law will condemn you who, even though you have the written code and circumcision, are a lawbreaker. Greater privilege brings greater condemnation when disobeyed. The Gentile who obeys exposes the hypocrisy of the disobedient Jew.

A person is not a Jew who is one only outwardly. True Jewish identity is not ethnic or external. God looks at the heart, not the genealogy. Outward religious identity without inward reality is a facade.

A person is a Jew who is one inwardly; circumcision is circumcision of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the written code. Inward transformation defines true covenant membership. Heart circumcision (Deuteronomy 10:16, 30:6) is accomplished by the Spirit, not human effort. Such a person's praise is not from other people, but from God. God's approval matters infinitely more than human approval.
